title: Is ‘Three Billboards’ Actually Just ‘Crash’ 2.0? Plus, Other Oscars Takes | The Big Picture (Ep. 52)
format: Two Oscar-week roundtables, pulled from The Ringer's video panels: whether 'Three Billboards' is 'Crash' 2.0, and whether a fish-sex movie can win Best Picture.

segments.heading: Is ‘Three Billboards’ just ‘Crash’ 2.0?
segments.note: Sean, Wesley, Amanda, Chris and Cam on Martin McDonagh's Best Picture nominee

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: The subject. The panel finds it counterfeit, a British-Irish playwright's algorithmic pass at race, gender and small-town America, with a last shot that retroactively exposes everything before it. Wesley grants Frances McDormand's performance but not the movie.

films.title: Crash
films.note: Paul Haggis's Best Picture winner, the benchmark for a film that thinks it's seeing America and isn't. Wesley rates Three Billboards worse in some ways, but says at least Crash is legibly, satisfyingly wrong.

films.title: In Bruges
films.note: McDonagh's debut, raised to argue his violence-and-absurdism register worked before the big-themes reach, and that its priest opening is playing with less dynamite.

films.title: Seven Psychopaths
films.note: McDonagh's second feature, same absurdist mode, held up as the frivolous company Three Billboards is being lifted out of.

films.title: Fargo
films.note: McDormand's first Oscar and the no-bullshit reputation the panel weighs against how hard she's campaigning here.

films.title: 12 Years a Slave
films.note: Wesley's counterexample: proof a non-American can tell a searingly true American story, which is the thing he says McDonagh can't.

films.title: La La Land
films.note: Last year's polarizing fight. Wesley calls it a fine movie that benefited from the discourse rather than being victimized by it.

segments.heading: Can a fish-sex movie win Best Picture?
segments.note: Cam out, Chris in: the panel handicaps 'The Shape of Water' and the field

films.title: The Shape of Water
films.note: The subject: del Toro's 13-nomination front-runner. Wesley is the lone defender ('this movie's congressman'); the rest call it well-made but out of step, the movie only people who make movies love, boosted by the preferential ballot and guild strength.

films.title: Pan's Labyrinth
films.note: Del Toro's real masterpiece, per the panel, lightly passed over in its time, which is why the Academy is belatedly rewarding him now.

films.title: The Departed
films.note: The analogy: a lesser 'karaoke' film that finally got Scorsese his Oscar. Shape of Water is cast as del Toro's Departed.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: The genre movie the couch would rather see win, and the model for a savvy year-long campaign.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Among the movies the panel likes more than the front-runner.

films.title: Phantom Thread
films.note: Sean's singular pick: a movie that hits one note hard instead of ticking every box.

films.title: The Post
films.note: The classic nominee the journalists on the couch liked and the field forgot; early buzz said just FedEx the statues to Spielberg, Hanks and Streep.

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: A onetime July front-runner that never got traction.

films.title: Darkest Hour
films.note: The 'dull' punchline of the group. Wesley bristles when it's called duller than a fish-sex movie.

films.title: Call Me by Your Name
films.note: Amanda's cause; pegged as likely first-out on the preferential ballot.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Two weeks into release. The panel games out whether audiences (not Marvel, which doesn't campaign) can hashtag it into next year's race the way voters hashtag people out.

films.title: Creed
films.note: Coogler's Rocky movie that Wesley thinks should have done better than it did.

films.title: Fruitvale Station
films.note: Coogler's Sundance breakout, once seemingly on a train to the Academy Awards.

films.title: Star Wars
films.note: The original 'people's slot' Best Picture nominee, the precedent for a beloved genre film breaking in.

films.title: Star Wars: The Last Jedi
films.note: Chris thought it might get nominated; the backlash discourse swallowed the conversation instead.

referenced.group: Elsewhere in the Best Picture mix

films: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King

The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the.title: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
